title: Null Values and Included Uniface Entities in XML
breadcrumbs: Null Values and Included Uniface Entities in XML
text: Null Values and Included Uniface Entities in XML Null fields are saved into XML streams as empty elements. This means that a null field is indistinguishable from an empty string. When an empty element is loaded into a numeric field it is converted into a zero numeric value. Null Values and Absent Elements Fields in occurrences created by xmlload that do not have an XML stream element mapped to them are treated as null fields. That is, when reconnect (or retrieve/reconnect ) merges the occurrences created from the XML stream with database occurrences, the null fields do not overwrite the database fields. Included entities are an exception to this behavior. Included Entities When reconnecting disconnected record sets, occurrences of included entities are completely overwritten by the data in the disconnected record. Therefore, if any data from an included entity is required in an XML stream, it is essential to include all the fields of the included entity in the XML stream. Note: If there is no element in the XML stream for a given field of an included entity, then that field is emptied. This means that if all the data of an included entity is not placed in an XML stream, data loss can occur when the XML stream is reconnected. This restriction applies to only included entities. Related Links XML Streams XML streams are data streams using an XML format. They enable loosely coupled connections between components, and provide a standardized format for data transport between components. Uniface can transfer field data and data relationships via XML stream parameters. It can also attach processing information to data in an XML stream to allow the data state to be transported with the data. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
